title: Hand tool edge care
summary: A maintained cutting edge requires less force and is easier to control, but sharpening itself creates cut, flying-particle, and tool-failure hazards. This card covers inspection, restrained maintenance, verification, and safe storage for non-powered hand tools.
content:
A maintained cutting edge requires less force and is easier to control, but sharpening itself creates cut, flying-particle, and tool-failure hazards. This card covers inspection, restrained maintenance, verification, and safe storage for non-powered hand tools.

Core procedure:
1. Identify the tool and its intended edge geometry; use the manufacturer’s maintenance instructions rather than guessing an angle or method.
2. Remove the tool from service and inspect the head, handle, fasteners, edge, and surrounding metal for cracks, looseness, mushrooming, deep chips, or corrosion.
3. Secure the tool so the edge faces away from the body and the work cannot slip; clear bystanders from the line of movement.
4. Use controlled strokes with the correct file or stone, preserving the original profile and removing only enough material to restore a continuous edge.
5. Remove loose filings, wipe and protect the surface as appropriate, then inspect for burrs, overheating marks, new cracks, or an uneven bevel.
6. Test control on suitable scrap material—not with a finger—then fit the guard, label any limitation, and record the date and work completed.

Primary safety boundary: Do not sharpen or use a tool with a cracked head, loose or split handle, badly chipped edge, missing guard, or unknown heat damage.

Fallback: If the correct sharpening tool or angle is unknown, clean and protect the item, mark it out of service, and refer it to a competent person.
materials: stable workbench or clamping fixture
manufacturer-approved file or sharpening stone
eye protection and task-appropriate gloves
clean cloth and light protective oil where suitable
edge guard, sheath, or labelled storage rack
maintenance log
steps: Identify the tool and its intended edge geometry; use the manufacturer’s maintenance instructions rather than guessing an angle or method.
Remove the tool from service and inspect the head, handle, fasteners, edge, and surrounding metal for cracks, looseness, mushrooming, deep chips, or corrosion.
Secure the tool so the edge faces away from the body and the work cannot slip; clear bystanders from the line of movement.
Use controlled strokes with the correct file or stone, preserving the original profile and removing only enough material to restore a continuous edge.
Remove loose filings, wipe and protect the surface as appropriate, then inspect for burrs, overheating marks, new cracks, or an uneven bevel.
Test control on suitable scrap material—not with a finger—then fit the guard, label any limitation, and record the date and work completed.
warnings: Do not sharpen or use a tool with a cracked head, loose or split handle, badly chipped edge, missing guard, or unknown heat damage.
Do not improvise high-speed grinding, change the original edge geometry, or work while the tool is unsupported or aimed toward another person.
common_mistakes: Testing sharpness by running a finger along the edge
Sharpening without first repairing a loose handle or damaged head
Removing too much metal or creating a thin overheated edge
Returning the tool to shared storage without a guard or status label
alternatives: If the correct sharpening tool or angle is unknown, clean and protect the item, mark it out of service, and refer it to a competent person.
Use a sound, correctly maintained replacement rather than forcing a damaged or blunt tool through the task.
failure_signs: The edge chips, rolls, cracks, or cannot hold its profile during a light scrap test
The handle, head, or fastener moves under hand pressure
Sharpening produces blue discoloration, uncontrolled heat, severe vibration, or flying fragments
when_not_to_use: Do not perform edge maintenance without the correct restraint, eye protection, lighting, and enough skill to follow the tool’s intended geometry.
Do not use this card for powered saws, grinding wheels, chainsaws, or specialised blades that require manufacturer servicing.
short_term: Inspect, restore, test, guard, and log one tool before returning it to service.
long_term: Maintain a tool register with inspection intervals, approved edge profiles, spare handles and guards, and named competent maintainers.
if_method_fails: Quarantine the tool, attach a clear defect note, and replace or professionally repair it; do not keep reshaping a failing edge.
environment_notes: Keep the bench dry and well lit, capture filings, protect others from the work zone, and prevent children from accessing tools or removed guards.

title: Manual pump care log
summary: A manual water pump should be checked by observation and recorded trends, not dismantled whenever output changes. This card creates a daily operating log, protects the water point from contamination, and defines when trained repair or water-quality action is required.
content:
A manual water pump should be checked by observation and recorded trends, not dismantled whenever output changes. This card creates a daily operating log, protects the water point from contamination, and defines when trained repair or water-quality action is required.

Core procedure:
1. Identify the pump, water source, responsible operator, manufacturer guidance, and the normal number of strokes or time needed for a measured container.
2. Before operation, inspect the platform, drain, cover, handle, fasteners, spout, surrounding ground, and nearby contamination risks without opening sealed parts.
3. Pump normally and record date, operator, weather, approximate output, unusual resistance, noise, leakage, colour, odour, or visible sediment.
4. Keep the platform and drain clear, prevent containers from touching the spout, and stop animals, waste water, fuel, chemicals, or latrine runoff from reaching the source area.
5. Compare the reading with earlier entries; mark small changes for review and immediately isolate sudden output loss, structural damage, or suspected contamination.
6. At handover, list open faults, spare parts requested, water-use restrictions, samples or authority contacts, and the next scheduled inspection.

Primary safety boundary: Do not assume clear-looking water is safe, and do not return a flooded, damaged, or contamination-suspected source to drinking use without competent assessment and required testing.

Fallback: If output is unreliable, close the point, post the reason, and distribute water from a separately verified safe source.
materials: weatherproof pump log and pencil
clean measuring container
watch or timer
inspection checklist for the exact pump model
cleaning brush and approved surface-cleaning supplies
out-of-service sign and barrier tape
steps: Identify the pump, water source, responsible operator, manufacturer guidance, and the normal number of strokes or time needed for a measured container.
Before operation, inspect the platform, drain, cover, handle, fasteners, spout, surrounding ground, and nearby contamination risks without opening sealed parts.
Pump normally and record date, operator, weather, approximate output, unusual resistance, noise, leakage, colour, odour, or visible sediment.
Keep the platform and drain clear, prevent containers from touching the spout, and stop animals, waste water, fuel, chemicals, or latrine runoff from reaching the source area.
Compare the reading with earlier entries; mark small changes for review and immediately isolate sudden output loss, structural damage, or suspected contamination.
At handover, list open faults, spare parts requested, water-use restrictions, samples or authority contacts, and the next scheduled inspection.
warnings: Do not assume clear-looking water is safe, and do not return a flooded, damaged, or contamination-suspected source to drinking use without competent assessment and required testing.
Do not open the wellhead, remove pump internals, add unapproved lubricant, or improvise parts unless trained for that exact pump and authorised to do so.
common_mistakes: Recording “working” without a measured output or observable condition
Cleaning the platform while allowing dirty water to drain back toward the source
Ignoring a gradual increase in strokes, leakage, or handle play
Mixing maintenance tools or lubricants with clean water containers
alternatives: If output is unreliable, close the point, post the reason, and distribute water from a separately verified safe source.
If no measuring vessel exists, use one dedicated clean container and record fill time consistently rather than estimating by eye.
failure_signs: Sudden loss of prime or output, severe handle looseness, cracked platform, open wellhead, or persistent abnormal noise
Floodwater, sewage, fuel, chemicals, dead animals, or heavy runoff near the source
New colour, odour, turbidity, sediment, illness cluster, or official notice questioning water safety
when_not_to_use: Do not use the pump for drinking water while contamination, structural damage, unsafe electrical equipment nearby, or an official restriction remains unresolved.
This log is not a repair manual and does not replace pump-specific maintenance instructions, trained technicians, water testing, or public-health direction.
short_term: Record one complete baseline check and clearly mark whether the source is open, restricted, or out of service.
long_term: Use dated trend records to schedule preventive maintenance, stock model-specific seals and fasteners, train at least two operators, and maintain a water-safety plan.
if_method_fails: Close and label the pump, protect the source from further contamination, notify the responsible water authority or trained repairer, and activate the verified alternate-water plan.
environment_notes: After flooding, earthquake damage, wildfire runoff, or chemical release, treat mechanical function and water safety as separate questions; a working pump can still deliver unsafe water.

title: Power bank cold-weather care
summary: Cold conditions can reduce the available output of a power bank, while charging a very cold, wet, damaged, or swollen lithium battery can create a serious failure risk. This card focuses on dry storage, gradual temperature recovery, inspection, conservative charging, and early isolation of warning signs.
content:
Cold conditions can reduce the available output of a power bank, while charging a very cold, wet, damaged, or swollen lithium battery can create a serious failure risk. This card focuses on dry storage, gradual temperature recovery, inspection, conservative charging, and early isolation of warning signs.

Core procedure:
1. Read the model’s permitted charging and storage temperatures; when those limits are unavailable, avoid charging while the unit is very cold or shows condensation.
2. Transport the power bank dry, protected from crushing and loose metal objects, and keep it close to the body or in an insulated bag without applying direct heat.
3. After entering a warmer area, leave the disconnected unit on a dry nonflammable surface until it has gradually warmed and all condensation has disappeared.
4. Inspect the case, ports, cable, and connectors for swelling, cracks, corrosion, liquid, unusual smell, looseness, or recalled-model information before use.
5. Charge only with compatible equipment in a visible, ventilated location away from bedding, paper, direct sun, heaters, and escape routes; check it periodically.
6. Record abnormal capacity loss or temperature, rotate healthy units rather than fully exhausting one, and store them according to the manufacturer’s state-of-charge guidance.

Primary safety boundary: Stop using or charging a power bank that is swollen, cracked, leaking, unusually hot, hissing, smoking, emitting vapour, or giving a strong unusual odour; move people away and call emergency services for fire or immediate danger.

Fallback: If the unit cannot be safely warmed and inspected, keep it disconnected and use a verified spare battery or reduce phone power use.
materials: manufacturer instructions and model label
dry insulated pouch that does not trap a charging device
approved undamaged charging cable and adapter
nonflammable clear charging surface
battery status and rotation log
separate fire-safe isolation area away from exits
steps: Read the model’s permitted charging and storage temperatures; when those limits are unavailable, avoid charging while the unit is very cold or shows condensation.
Transport the power bank dry, protected from crushing and loose metal objects, and keep it close to the body or in an insulated bag without applying direct heat.
After entering a warmer area, leave the disconnected unit on a dry nonflammable surface until it has gradually warmed and all condensation has disappeared.
Inspect the case, ports, cable, and connectors for swelling, cracks, corrosion, liquid, unusual smell, looseness, or recalled-model information before use.
Charge only with compatible equipment in a visible, ventilated location away from bedding, paper, direct sun, heaters, and escape routes; check it periodically.
Record abnormal capacity loss or temperature, rotate healthy units rather than fully exhausting one, and store them according to the manufacturer’s state-of-charge guidance.
warnings: Stop using or charging a power bank that is swollen, cracked, leaking, unusually hot, hissing, smoking, emitting vapour, or giving a strong unusual odour; move people away and call emergency services for fire or immediate danger.
Do not heat a cold battery with flame, stove, radiator, hot water, hair dryer, or improvised heater, and do not charge it inside a sealed pouch or sleeping area.
common_mistakes: Charging immediately after bringing a frozen or condensation-covered unit indoors
Keeping keys, coins, or damaged cables against exposed connectors
Assuming rapid capacity loss is always harmless cold-weather behaviour
Charging unattended, under bedding, or in an evacuation route
alternatives: If the unit cannot be safely warmed and inspected, keep it disconnected and use a verified spare battery or reduce phone power use.
If no safe charging area exists, prioritise emergency communications and delay charging until a visible nonflammable location is available.
failure_signs: Swelling, case separation, leakage, corrosion, smoke, vapour, hissing, or a sharp chemical smell
Unexpected heating while idle or during a low-power charge
Repeated shutdowns, damaged ports, sparking, or capacity collapse after gradual warming
when_not_to_use: Do not reconnect a wet, physically damaged, recalled, or previously overheated power bank.
Do not attempt to open, puncture, flatten, freeze, repair, or dispose of a lithium battery in household fire or ordinary rubbish; follow local hazardous-battery instructions.
short_term: Warm one disconnected unit gradually, inspect it, complete one supervised charge if allowed, and record the result.
long_term: Rotate labelled power banks, retain model instructions and recall details, inspect cables, and store units dry within manufacturer limits.
if_method_fails: Disconnect power if safe, clear people from the area, do not handle a smoking or burning unit, call emergency services, and use the verified communications backup.
environment_notes: Wind chill does not cool an enclosed battery like exposed skin, but prolonged low temperature and condensation still matter; keep the unit dry and follow its documented limits.
